coredata.dc:title: IR780 loaded perfluorohexane nanodroplets for efficient sonodynamic effect induced by short-pulsed focused ultrasound
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Nan
Qin, Dui
Wu, Pengying
Xu, Shanshan
Wang, Supin
Wan, Mingxi
coredata.dc:description: Inertial cavitation is crucial for the therapeutic effects of sonodynamic. Therefore, approaches that can induce highly efficient inertial cavitation should be of benefit for sonodynamic effect. Our previous study demonstrated that highly efficient inertial cavitation activity can be achieved through the combinatorial use of a short-pulsed focused ultrasound (SPFU) sequence and perfluorohexane (PFH) nanodroplets. Herein, we applied the SPFU sequence and PFH nanodroplets in sonodynamic. A hydrophobic sonosensitizer, IR780 iodine, was loaded inside denatured bovine serum albumin-shelled PFH (PFH@BSA-IR780) nanodroplets. The sonodynamic efficacy was validated by treating HeLa cervical cancer cells. Under SPFU exposure, PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets were highly effective in promoting reactive oxygen species generation and inducing cancer cell death. A significant decrease in cell viability was achieved within just 10 s. Besides the cytotoxicity of ROS, the mechanical bioeffects of inertial cavitation also led to severe cell death resulting from higher acoustic power or the longer treatment time. The application of the SPFU sequence coupled with PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets is a promising strategy for efficient sonodynamic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sonodynamic effect
Inertial cavitation
Short-pulsed focused ultrasound
Perfluorohexane nanodroplet

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent staining, cell viability assay and flow cytometry
Detection of ROS and hydroxyl radicals

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The size distribution of the newly fabricated PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets. The inserted graph is the TEM image of the droplets. (b) The mean diameter variation of the PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets over time (24 h) at physiological temperature (37 °C). (c) The absorption spectra (450–1000 nm) of IR780 DMF solution, PFH@BSA nanodroplets, and PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets. (d) Cytotoxicity test of the PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets at different volume concentrations with and without SPFU (*<0.05).

(a) The SCL images from different groups. The white arrow indicates the propagation direction of ultrasound. (b) The corresponding SCL intensity and area (*<0.05). (c) The ICD as a function of treatment time.
The bright field and the merged PI and Calcein-AM fluorescent staining microscopic images from different groups. Greens are the viable cells stained by calcein. Red are dead cells stained by PI.

(a) The flow cytometry results from different groups. (b) The ROS and
HO production detected by KI and TA after different treatments (*<0.05 versus control, #<0.05 versus SPFU + BSA). (c) The cell viability measured by the CCK-8 assay (*<0.05 versus control, #<0.05 versus SPFU + BSA). (d) The correlation between ROS amount and cell viability.
The merged PI and Calcein-AM fluorescent staining images after treatment at different acoustic powers. The white arrows indicate the red clusters.

(a) The total cell numbers in the microscopic images after treatment at different acoustic powers. (b) The ICD as a function of treatment time at different acoustic powers. (c) The ROS and
HO production detected by KI and TA at different acoustic powers. (d) The cell viabilities at different acoustic powers.

(a) The merged PI and Calcein-AM fluorescent staining images after different treatment durations. (b) The ROS and
HO production detected by KI and TA after different treatment durations. (c) The ICD as a function of treatment time. (d) The cell viabilities after different treatment durations.
The cell viabilities after sonication at 4.5 and 6 W in the presence of NAC with different concentrations (0–5 mM).

head.ce:title: IR780 loaded perfluorohexane nanodroplets for efficient sonodynamic effect induced by short-pulsed focused ultrasound

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education, Department of Biomedical Engineering, School of Life Science and Technology, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an 710049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education
School of Life Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inertial cavitation is crucial for the therapeutic effects of sonodynamic. Therefore, approaches that can induce highly efficient inertial cavitation should be of benefit for sonodynamic effect. Our previous study demonstrated that highly efficient inertial cavitation activity can be achieved through the combinatorial use of a short-pulsed focused ultrasound (SPFU) sequence and perfluorohexane (PFH) nanodroplets. Herein, we applied the SPFU sequence and PFH nanodroplets in sonodynamic. A hydrophobic sonosensitizer, IR780 iodine, was loaded inside denatured bovine serum albumin-shelled PFH (PFH@BSA-IR780) nanodroplets. The sonodynamic efficacy was validated by treating HeLa cervical cancer cells. Under SPFU exposure, PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets were highly effective in promoting reactive oxygen species generation and inducing cancer cell death. A significant decrease in cell viability was achieved within just 10 s. Besides the cytotoxicity of ROS, the mechanical bioeffects of inertial cavitation also led to severe cell death resulting from higher acoustic power or the longer treatment time. The application of the SPFU sequence coupled with PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets is a promising strategy for efficient sonodynamic.

ce:section.ce:para: Sonodynamic effect was firstly discovered in 1989 by Yumita et al. [1] and termed as ‘sonodynamic’ in 1992 [2]. It involves ultrasound activation of a chemical agent, called sonosensitizer, to generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS). ROS are associated with detrimental effects for cancer cells, including membrane and cytoskeletal damage, DNA fragmentation, and loss of mitochondrial membrane potential [3–5], as well as having an effect on the tumor microenvironment, for example, through the inhibition of tumor angiogenesis [6,7]. Further, sonodynamic offers the advantage of tissue penetration for the non-invasive treatment of deeply-seated tumors [8]. Thus, it provides a low selectivity toward a wide range of cancers compared to photodynamic therapy [9,10]. A large volume of literature is available on the possible mechanisms, novel sonosensitizers, and potential applications of sonodynamic [4,11].

Although the exact mechanisms behind the therapeutic effects of sonodynamic remain unclear, it has been generally accepted that inertial cavitation is crucial for its efficacy [12,13]. Thus, approaches able to induce effective inertial cavitation should be of benefit to the sonodynamic efficiency. Few studies have focused on the inertial cavitation property during sonodynamic, with most using ultrasound sequences in continuous mode, which do not favor the generation of efficient inertial cavitation. Yasuda et al. proposed a combinatorial ultrasound sequence (trigger pulse plus sustaining waves) for highly efficient inertial cavitation and ROS production [14]. Indeed, this combinatorial ultrasound sequence has proved effective in treating colon and pancreatic cancers in mouse models through sonodynamic [15].

Our previous study demonstrated that efficient inertial cavitation can be induced by a short-pulsed focused ultrasound (SPFU) sequence with an extremely short pulse duration (10 μs) and a relatively high pulse repetition frequency [16]. Through the use of perfluorohexane (PFH) nanodroplets as additional cavitation nuclei, the inertial cavitation activity can be further enhanced [17]. Herein, we applied a SPFU sequence and PFH nanodroplets in sonodynamic to achieve a highly efficient outcome. Additionally, PFH nanodroplets were also employed as carriers of the hydrophobic sonosensitizer IR780 iodide [18].

The biodistribution of hydrophobic sonosensitizers in vivo is ubiquitous, with prolonged clearance from normal tissues [19], which complicate the application of sonodynamic therapy without undesired side-effects to healthy tissue positioned between the ultrasound source and the target [13]. Therefore, the encapsulation of these sonosensitizers within carriers provides a practical way for addressing this critical issue. Various types of micro- and nanoparticles have been developed as nanosensitizers or sonosensitizer vehicles [20]. For IR780, a recent study used a hollow mesoporous organosilica nanoparticle as its carrier and succeeded in treating pancreatic cancer cells through sonodynamic effect [21]. Herein, IR780 was loaded inside denatured bovine serum albumin (BSA)-shelled PFH (PFH@BSA-IR780) nanodroplets through hydrophobic interactions with BSA [22].

Upon the irradiation of ultrasound with sufficient negative pressure, PFH nanodroplets can be acoustically vaporized into microbubbles specifically in the regions where superharmonic focusing occurs [23]; this process is termed acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV) [24]. ADV can be of benefit for treatment accuracy, wherein ADV microbubbles can act as cavitation nuclei, undergoing violent oscillation and collapse (inertial cavitation). During the collapse phase of inertial cavitation, extremely high temperatures and pressures can be generated [25] to trigger a series of chemical reactions and stimulate the sonosensitizer into the excited state [12]. ROS can be generated from the cavitation-induced pyrolysis of water and the energy transition of the excited sonosensitizer to the ground state. According to our previous study, PFH-core nanodroplets are able to generate more controllable inertial cavitation activity because of the relatively high ADV threshold of PFH [17]. Additionally, inertial cavitation only emerged near the focal region of the focused transducer, further improving the treatment accuracy. Moreover, PFH also possesses an efficient oxygen-carrying capacity able to supply enough oxygen for ROS production [26]. In aerobic environments, ROS is invariably produced through ultrasound irradiation [5]. Furthermore, ADV bubbles also possess high echogenicity for acoustic imaging, providing the possibility to monitor the treatment process in situ [26].

To testify the efficacy and efficiency of our proposed method, HeLa cervical cancer cells were chosen as treatment targets. The cells were contained within an acoustically transparent tube and sonicated by the SPFU sequence in the presence of PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets. The ROS production region was visualized using sonochemiluminescence (SCL) and the amount of ROS was quantified by the SCL light intensity and potassium iodide (KI) oxidization method. The hydroxyl radicals (
OH) were detected by terephthalic acid (TA). The inertial cavitation activity was monitored by passive cavitation detection during sonication. The cancer cell death was assayed by fluorescent staining, Cell Count Kit-8 (CCK-8), and flow cytometry. Moreover, the effects of acoustic power, treatment time, and ROS scavenger on the treatment outcome were also investigated. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report assessing SPFU sequence-induced sonodynamic effect mediated by nanosized PFH droplets.

The SPFU sequence consisted of pulsed waves with an extremely short pulse duration of 10 μs and a pulse repetition frequency of 2 kHz (Fig. 1 ), emitted from a concave, single-element focused ultrasound transducer (Imasonic, France). The pulsed waveform was generated from channel one of a double-channel arbitrary wave generator (AWG420, Tektronix, USA), then amplified using a linear power amplifier (AG1017, T&G Power Conversion, USA), and finally fed to the transducer (Fig. 1). The working frequency of the transducer was 1.2 MHz, corresponding to a transmission efficiency (acoustic power/electric power) of 65%. The full-width half-maximum beam lengths on the axial and lateral directions of the transducer were 8 and 1.2 mm, respectively, which were usually considered as the focal region. The characteristics of acoustic pressure distribution in the ultrasound field have been described in detail in our previous study [27]. The transducer was mounted on one side of an acrylic tank (50 × 28 × 28 cm) filled with degassed water (<2 mg/L dissolved oxygen, 30 °C) as the acoustic propagation medium. On the other side of the tank, an acoustic absorber (acoustic absorptivity, 0.538) was attached to reduce the acoustic reflection. An acoustically transparent tube containing cells was immersed in the degassed water and linked to a three-axis positioning device (NFP, Zolix, China) to adjust its location.

The fabrication process of the PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets followed that described by Jiang et al. [22], with some alterations according to the current need. Briefly, BSA (MackLin, China) was dissolved into degassed Milli-Q filtered water to prepare a 2 mg/mL solution, to which 2-mercaptoethanol was added to expose the hydrophobic region of BSA. IR780 iodine (Alfa Aesar, UK) dissolved in dimethylformamide (DMF) (0.4 mg/mL) was mixed with the denatured BSA solution, followed by addition of 1.5% (v/v) PFH (Sigma-Aldrich, USA). The solution was then emulsified with an ultrasonic liquid processor (VCX 750, Sonic & Materials, USA) for 1 min at an amplitude of 20% and a duty cycle of 80% to produce a uniform distribution. The entire solution was cooled in an ice bath. The obtained green dispersion was centrifuged at 1000 r/min for 10 min and the suspension was poured out to remove any unreacted BSA, IR780, DMF, and 2-mercaptoethanol. The sediment (nanodroplets) was then dissolved into degassed Milli-Q filtered water. Control nanodroplets (PFH@BSA) without IR780 addition were prepared through an identical process albeit without the addition of IR780 DMF solution.

The initial diameters of the stabilized nanodroplets were determined by dynamic light scattering using the Zetasizer nanosizing system (Malvern, UK). The size stability of the nanodroplets was tested by keeping the nanodroplets within a 37 °C water bath over 24 h. The morphology of the fabricated nanodroplets was observed by TEM (Hitachi, Japan). Optical absorption spectra of the IR780 DMF solution, PFH@BSA nanodroplets, and PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Purkinje general, China) to assess the degree of IR780 loading. The encapsulation efficiency and loading efficiency were calculated as follows:
Encapsulation efficiency
%
=
weight of loaded IR
780
/
weight of initially added IR
780
× 100 %
Loading efficiency
%
=
weight of loaded IR
780
/
weight of nanodroplets
× 100 %

HeLa cervical cancer cells (CCL-2, ATCC, USA) were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium (HyClone, Thermo Scientific, USA) supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (HyClone, Thermo Scientific, USA) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin (Invitrogen, USA) in a humidified incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2 atmosphere. Prior to treatment, the cells were trypsinized from the culture plate, centrifuged, and redistributed within the acoustically transparent tubes. The used medium was replaced with degassed fresh culture medium. Subsequently, the cells were randomly divided into the following four groups: (1) control group (Control), (2) ultrasound only (SPFU), (3) ultrasound and 1% PFH@BSA nanodroplets (SPFU + BSA), and (4) ultrasound and 1% PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets (SPFU + BSA-IR780). After treatment, the cells were seeded into 24 or 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h prior to flow cytometry, or fluorescent staining and cell viability assay.

A luminol solution was prepared using luminol (3-aminophthalhydrazide, 0.01 M), sodium carbonate, and degassed Milli-Q filtered water, and used for SCL experiments. To exclude the influence of cells on ROS generation, an SCL image was captured in a cell-free system. The newly prepared luminol solution was divided into four groups as mentioned in section 2.2. The light acquisition experiment was conducted in a lightproof box (only for SCL experiment) using an EMCCD camera (iXon3 897, Andor Technology Plc., UK), synchronously triggered by channel two of the wave generator (Fig. 1). The total acquisition time for SCL was 10 s. The photons emitted from SCL were collected by the array detector, then converted to electrons and further multiplied using the EM gain register. The images were post-processed using Andor Solis software (Andor technology PLC, UK) to determine the total counts of SCL photons, which is closely related to the ROS amount. The luminous area in the SCL image was calculated using Matlab (MathWorks, USA) after transferring the original SCL image into a binarized image. The luminous region reveals the spatial distribution of the oxidization sites within the acoustic field.

The inertial cavitation dose (ICD) was calculated from the back-scattered acoustic signals received by a single-element passive cavitation detection transducer (V309, Panametrics, USA), which was placed at the center of the focused ultrasound transducer (Fig. 1). The bandwidth of this transducer was 2.15–6.9 MHz, with a center frequency of 5 MHz. The received acoustic emissions were collected using a high-speed digitizer (5122, National Instruments, USA) with 14-bit resolution and 100-MHz bandwidth. Following the removal of harmonics, subharmonics, and ultraharmonics using a comb-like filter, the spectral bands between 0.1 and 10 MHz were extracted, and the root-mean-square value was calculated to measure the ICD. The root-mean-square amplitudes were then plotted as a function of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent staining, cell viability assay and flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence assay consists of propidium iodide (PI, P-4864, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and Calcein-AM (C3100MP, Invitrogen, USA) staining. After treatment and 24 h incubation in a 96-well plate, Calcein-AM was added at a final concentration of 1 μM as a viable cell stain. Calcein-AM can passively cross cell membranes and be converted to calcein (excitation/emission of 488/520 nm) only in living cells [28]. After further incubation for 15 min in the humidified incubator, PI was added to the medium at a final concentration of 100 μM. PI is a non-fluorescent, cell-impermeant intercalating agent that exhibits strong fluorescence when bound to DNA/RNA (excitation/emission of 535/617 nm). Fluorescence imaging was performed using a Digital Sight DS-U2 camera (Nikon, Japan) connected to an inverted fluorescence microscope (TiU, Nikon, Japan) equipped with a 20 × objective (PlanFluor, Nikon, Japan). A mercury lamp assembled with suitable fluorescent filters was used for fluorescence imaging.

Cell viability was quantified by the CCK-8 (Dojindo Laboratories, Japan) assay according to the manufacturer’s instructions. After treatment and 24 h incubation in a 96-well plate, a 10% (v/v) CCK-8 solution was added to the culture medium and incubated for a further 90 min. The absorbance was measured at 450 nm by using a microplate absorbance reader (SpectraMax 190, Molecular Devices, USA) in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions. The cell viability was calculated as follows:
Cell
viability
%
=
absorbance
of
treated
group
- blank
control
no
cell
/
absorbance
of
control
group - blank
control
no
cell
× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ROS and hydroxyl radicals
ce:section.ce:para:
To detect the ROS or
OH, 1 M KI (Aladdin, Shanghai, China) or 10−4 M TA (Aladdin, Shanghai, China) was added prior to sonication. ROS can oxidize I− and generate I3 −, which has a peak absorption at 350 nm [14]. The absorbance was measured using a microplate absorbance reader (SpectraMax 190, Molecular Devices, USA). TA can specifically react with
OH to form 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA), that gives a bright stable fluorescence (excitation/emission of 315/425 nm) [29]. The fluorescence intensity was also measured using the microplate absorbance reader.

The obtained data were expressed as the mean value ± standard deviation (SD). The Student’s t-test (two-tailed) was used to establish the statistical significance between two individual groups and p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. All statistical analysis was performed using SPSS (IBM, USA).

The mean diameter of the newly fabricated PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets was 197.6 ± 51.49 nm (Fig. 2 a), whereas that of PFH@BSA nanodroplets without IR780 was 191.2 ± 44.14 nm. TEM observation also showed the diameter of the PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets to be approximately 200 nm. The PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets size at human physiological temperature (37 °C) over 24 h remained stable, without agglomeration (Fig. 2b). Previous studies on the in vivo distribution of the perfluorocarbon nanodroplets reported that nanodroplets with diameters of approximately 200 nm can passively accumulate in the well-perfused region of tumor tissue through the enhanced permeation and retention effect [30,31]. Moreover, ultrasound can also enhance the dispersion of agents through the impermeable regions of a tumor [32,33]. Therefore, the synthesized PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets with diameters of approximately 200 nm appear to be sufficiently stable for their future application in in vivo studies.

The encapsulation efficiency of IR780 in the nanodroplets was 69.86 ± 3.62%, while the loading efficiency was 0.6 ± 0.007%. The UV–vis absorbance spectra (from 450 to 1000 nm) of the IR780 DMF solution, the PFH@BSA nanodroplets, and the PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets were measured (Fig. 2c). BSA-IR780 nanodroplets showed characteristic peaks of both BSA nanodroplets and IR780, demonstrating that IR780 was successfully encapsulated within the nanodroplets. Compared with the spectrum of IR780 DMF solution, the peak absorbance of the PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets right-shifted to 787 nm, likely due to hydrophobic interactions, as described by Jiang et al. [22].

To assess the cytotoxicity of PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets, HeLa cells were incubated with the nanodroplets at different volume concentrations (0.1–2%) for 24 h. No significant cell death was observed compared with the control group, indicating that the nanodroplets alone did not induce any cell damage. Upon irradiation with a SPFU sequence (6 W, 10 s), a significant decrease in cell viability was observed at volume concentrations above 0.5%. Furthermore, a decrease in cell viability was observed with increasing volume concentration, attributed to the increased inertial cavitation and IR780 amount. The roles of inertial cavitation and IR780 will be discussed in detail in the following sections.

Due to the significant role of ROS in sonodynamic cytotoxicity, it is necessary to acknowledge their distribution information during sonication. SCL was used to reveal the ROS generation region and amount in a cell-free system [14]. Luminol can be oxidized by ROS and emit light. Due to the high reactivity and short half-life (0.01–10 μs) of ROS, their diffusion radius is less than 20 nm [5,34]. Therefore, only the luminol molecules close to the ROS generation site can be oxidized and emit light, meaning that the ROS distribution area can be accurately revealed by the luminous region. Furthermore, the chemiluminescence intensity is directly related to the oxidization reactions and subsequently with the ROS amount. The SCL images (Fig. 3 a) and the corresponding light intensity and area (Fig. 3b), showing that no light radiated from the control group without ultrasound treatment. Upon the application of the SPFU sequence, a very weak and tiny luminous spot was captured at the acoustic power of 4.5 W. Yet, once the PFH@BSA nanodroplets were added as cavitation nuclei, the light intensity and luminous region became much brighter and larger due to the more intense inertial cavitation activity, as indicated by an increase in ICD (Fig. 3c). Additionally, both the SCL intensity and area (Fig. 3b) further increased significantly in the presence of IR780-loaded nanodroplets. Conversely, there was no significant difference in ICD between the groups with and without IR780, demonstrating that, firstly, loading IR780 into the nanodroplets did not influence their inertial cavitation property, and secondly, the increase in SCL did not originate from the more intensive inertial cavitation but rather from the increase in ROS in the presence of IR780.

To verify the function of the increase in ROS, HeLa cells were mixed with 1% (v/v) degassed Milli-Q filtered water, PFH@BSA nanodroplets, or PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets and sealed inside the acoustically transparent tubes before exposure. The SPFU sequence was then applied and sustained for 10 s at an acoustic power of 4.5 W. The bright field and fluorescent staining microscopic pictures from different groups were shown in Fig. 4 . The surviving cells with intact cell membrane and active intracellular esterases can remove the acetomethoxy group of calcein-AM, trap the calcein inside, and emit strong green fluorescence. At the same time, PI, DNA stain, can only permeate into dead cells with no intact cell membrane and give out red fluorescence when combined with DNA. As can be seen in the fluorescent images, the control group had a minimal number of dead cells. Meanwhile, no obvious viability decrease for cells with only 1% PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets as shown in Fig. 2(d). Once ultrasound was applied, inertial cavitation occurred, as indicated by the SCL observed. However, due to the lack of cavitation nuclei, the inertial cavitation activity in the SPFU group was too weak to induce obvious cell death. Only a minimum decrease in cell number was observed compared with that in the control group, likely arising from cell lysis by the mechanical disruption. Similar phenomenon was also observed in the presence of PFH@BSA nanodroplets, except for an obvious increase in dead cells (red). Many viable cells (green) were still visible in the SPFU + BSA group. With PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets, a minimum number of viable cells were observed after sonication. Additionally, a change in cellular morphology to a rounder shape and a smaller size than viable cells was observed in the dead cells.

The results of flow cytometry from different groups were shown in Fig. 5 (a). Similar with the fluorescence staining results, there is no significant difference in apoptotic rate between the control group and the SPFU group. With PFH@BSA nanodroplets, the apoptotic rate markedly increased. Further, with the IR780 in the nanodroplets, the apoptotic cell populations of HeLa cells significantly increased compared to that in the SPFU + BSA group. In all the four groups, no obvious cell necrosis occurred, which means that apoptosis is the main reason for cancer cell death in this experiment condition. Two factors can induce apoptosis during the sonication. One is the oxidative stress from ROS, and another is the mechanical stress from cavitation or ultrasound irradiation. ROS play a significant role in the regulation of the main pathways of apoptosis mediated by mitochondria, death receptors, and the endoplasmic reticulum [35]. The mechanical effects of ultrasound and cavitation include acoustic radiation fore, shock waves, and micro-jets [36]. These mechanical effects can cause cytoskeleton remolding, membrane damage, and DNA double-strand breaks, finally induce apoptosis. Investigations on ultrasound-induced apoptosis were reported without ROS involved [37–40].

The absorbance of I3 − and the fluorescence intensity of HTA were shown in Fig. 5(b). Similar to SCL intensity, almost no absorption or fluorescence was detected in the control group. Slight increases were observed when SPFU was applied. Significant increases in ROS and
OH production occurred when nanodroplets were added as cavitation nuclei. Further, in the presence of IR780, ROS and
OH production dramatically increased compared to that of the SPFU + BSA group. The cell viabilities of the different groups after 24 h incubation were shown in Fig. 5(c). The cell viability corresponded well with ROS production, with a negative correlation (Fig. 5d). This negative correlation indicated that ROS was the main factor for cancer cell death in this experiment condition. The main function of sonosensitizer in sonodynamic is to aid the generation of more ROS.

When the SPFU sequence was applied in the presence of PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets, ROS were generated from two processes. First, the nanodroplets can serve as cavitation nuclei undergoing violent oscillation and collapse. The implodes of inertial cavitation bubbles generated extremely high temperatures and pressures at the center of the collapsing bubbles, which can be view as a nanometric sonochemical reactor, able to generate ROS by the hemolytic cleavage of water molecules. Meanwhile, the energy radiated from the collapsing bubbles could be transferred to the sonosensitizers (IR780) and stimulate them into the excited state. When the excited sonosensitizers returned to their ground state, the released energy can be transferred to the circumambient oxygen to generate a large amount of ROS. Therefore, with intensive inertial cavitation occurring in the presence of PFH@BSA nanodroplets, the cell viability was 58.7%, yet it was only 10% with the addition of sonosensitizer IR780.
From the above results, it can be concluded that highly efficient sonodynamic was achieved in the presence of IR780-loaded PFH nanodroplets upon irradiation with the SPFU sequence for just 10 s.

Besides ROS-induced apoptosis, the mechanical destructions arising from inertial cavitation bubbles can also cause significant cell death [41]. To elucidate the influence of the mechanical destructions on cancer cells in sonodynamic, HeLa cells were exposed to SPFU at different acoustic powers for 10 s in the presence of 1% PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets. Following 24 h of incubation, fluorescence staining assay was conducted as shown in Fig. 6 . No obvious differences were observed between the cells in the control group and those treated at 3 W. When the power was raised to 6 W, a greater number of dead cells were visible along with an obvious decrease in total cell number. Cell numbers within the microscopic region were counted following irradiation at different acoustic powers (Fig. 7 a), and the total cell number decreased with increasing acoustic power. When the acoustic power increased above 3 W, the ICD increased with increasing treatment time as shown in Fig. 7(b). Similar observations were reported in our previous study when intensive inertial cavitation occurred in the presence of PFH nanodroplets [17]. Further, the ICD increased with increasing acoustic power. Higher ICD was related to more intensive inertial cavitation activity, which can bring about violent mechanical damage to cancer cells and lead to severe cell lysis as indicated by the dramatic cell number decrease. Hiraoka et al. investigated the role of mechanical effects in sonodynamic through a comparison of the therapeutic effects of sonodynamic and photodynamic using the same sensitizer [42]. Their results suggested that the enhanced ultrasound-induced cell death compared with photodynamic therapy was attributed to the mechanical stress of ultrasound. Yamaguchi et al. demonstrated that sonosensitizers can also result in physical destabilization of the cell membrane, making the cancer cells more susceptible to shear forces [43]. In the fluorescent images of 9 W, many red clusters (indicated by the white arrows in Fig. 6) were observed. Due to the fact that PI is a DNA stain, it can be deduced that these red clusters indicate DNA leakage from the ruptured cells.

The absorbance of I3 − and the fluorescence intensity of HTA were shown in Fig. 7(c). At a sonication acoustic power of 3 W, intensive inertial cavitation was not triggered as indicated by the ICD amplitude. Accordingly, no significant amount of ROS/
OH were generated. Increasing the acoustic power triggered more intensive inertial cavitation and more ROS/
OH production from the imploded cavitation bubbles. The cell viability at different acoustic powers with and without PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets (Fig. 7d) showed that an increase in acoustic power (above 3 W) alone, without the need for PFH nanodroplets, can also result in a slight decrease in cell viability. In the presence of PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets, there was a much greater decrease due to the significantly activated inertial cavitation. Both the mechanical bioeffects and ROS production depend on the existence of intensive inertial cavitation. The mechanical disruption-induced cell lysis became dominant in killing cancer cells at higher acoustic power.

In addition to mechanical damages, inertial cavitation can also significantly enhance in situ thermal deposition. If the temperature elevation is well controlled and maintained, this mild hyperthermia (39–43 °C) could increase tumor blood flow and vascular permeability [44], thereby assisting drug delivery [45], which also could benefit the therapeutic efficiency of sonodynamic [46].

Treatment time is another important factor likely to greatly influence the sonodynamic effect. According to the reported studies on sonodynamic effect, treatment time is usually in the order of minutes. Herein, the time was significantly shortened due to the highly efficient inertial cavitation triggered by the SPFU sequence and PFH nanodroplets. In order to assess the influence of treatment time, fluorescence assays were performed after irradiation HeLa cells for varying time at the acoustic power of 6 W (Fig. 8 a).

For a 5 s sonication time, the total cell number within the microscopic view was obviously reduced compared to the control group, with a minimal number of red cells, suggesting that cell number reduction was mainly due to cell lysis from mechanical disruption. Accordingly, the amount of ROS and
OH also showed a minimal production (Fig. 8b). Further, the ICD increased with increasing treatment time (Fig. 8c), meaning that the inertial cavitation activity became more intensive over time. Within the first 5 s, the amplitude was relatively low; correspondingly, the inertial cavitation activity was too weak to induce abundant ROS. As the treatment time increased to 10 s, the cavitation activity became more intensive and the ROS/
OH amount also significantly increased. Further extending the treatment time can yield more ROS and
OH. Nevertheless, the total cell number within the microscopic view dramatically decreased indicating the dominant effect of mechanical disruption with longer treatment time. Similar to the results in section 3.3, red clusters were observed (Fig. 8a) following an extension of treatment time to 15 s, speculated to be due to DNA leakage from the destructed cells. When the treatment time was set to 30 s, only two viable cells were observed, and the rest seemed to be totally eliminated. The CCK-8 assay results also showed a similar tendency, wherein, in the absence of nanodroplets, the cell viability did not change much compared to the control group following ultrasound alone. Once the nanodroplets were added, a significant decrease in cell viability occurred, accompanied by intensive inertial cavitation.

To further assess the role of ROS in sonodynamic cytotoxicity, the influence of an antioxidant compound N-acetyl cysteine (NAC) was tested at the acoustic power of 4.5 and 6 W. NAC has been widely used as a ROS scavenger in the field of apoptosis research for investigating the role of ROS in induction of apoptosis [47]. The HeLa cells were pretreated with NAC of different concentrations (0.5–5 mM), and then 1% PFH@BSA-PFH nanodroplets were added prior to sonication. Following irradiation by the SPFU sequence for 10 s, the cell viability 24 h later was measured by the CCK-8 assay. From the results in section 3.2 and 3.3, it has been shown that ROS-induced cell death was dominant when the acoustic power was 4.5 W, while at 6 W, the influence of the mechanical disruption became more severe as the ICD increased. The function of NAC is to protect the cells from the cytotoxicity of ROS. Therefore, it can be hypothesized that cell death by mechanical effects would not be affected.

After pretreated by NAC, the cell viability after sonodynamic treatment markedly increased as shown in Fig. 9 . Meanwhile, higher NAC concentration (2 mM) brought higher cell viability. However, when the NAC concentration was increased to 5 mM, the cell viability at both 4.5 and 6 W did not increase anymore. Since NAC can only inhibit the ROS cytotoxicity, cell death from the mechanical effects was not affected. This partially inhibition phenomenon further verified that the therapeutic effects of SPFU and PFH nanodroplet-mediated sonodynamic were achieved by the synergistic effect of ROS and mechanical damage from inertial cavitation. The influence of NAC was greater at 4.5 W compared to 6 W since the mechanical effects were stronger at higher acoustic power.

Our study proposed an efficient method to achieve sonodynamic effect through the combinatorial use of an SPFU sequence and PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets. The hydrophobic sonosensitizer IR780 iodine was successfully loaded within the denatured BSA-shelled PFH nanodroplets fabricated with diameters of approximately 200 nm through hydrophobic interactions. Upon SPUF irradiation at an acoustic power of 4.5 W, a large amount of ROS was generated in the presence of PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets, inducing dramatic HeLa cancer cell death for just 10 s. In addition to cell death induced by ROS, the mechanical damage directly arising from inertial cavitation can also lead to severe cancer cell loss. Moreover, the mechanical destruction become dominant in cancer cell killing with increasing acoustic power or treatment time. These results suggest that the SPFU sequence coupled with PFH@BSA-IR780 nanodroplets provides an efficient way for achieving sonodynamic effect.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFC0100701), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11474229 and 81771854).
